titleStmt.title: Fluctuation Spectroscopy in Granular Superconductors with Application to Boron-doped Nanocrystalline Diamond

funder.orgName: UK Engineering and Physical Sciences Rsearch Council

analytic.title: Fluctuation Spectroscopy in Granular Superconductors with Application to Boron-doped Nanocrystalline Diamond

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We perform a detailed calculation of the various contributions to the fluctuation conductivity of a granular metal close to its superconducting transition. We find three distinct regions of power law behavior in reduced temperature, О· = (T -T c )/T c , with crossovers at О“/T c and E T h /T c , where О“ is the electron tunneling rate, and E T h is the Thouless energy of a grain. The calculation includes both intergrain and intragrain degrees of freedom. This complete theory of the fluctuation region in granular superconductors is then compared to experimental results from boron-doped nanocrystalline diamond, using the assumption of a constant phase breaking rate, П„ -1 ПҶ . We find a semi-quantitative agreement between the theoretical and experimental results only in the case of large phase breaking. We argue that there may be a novel phase breaking mechanism in granular metals worthy of further experimental and theoretical investigation.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Electronic transport in granular materials has been studied both theoretically and experimentally in great depth since the 1960s, with earlier works focussing heavily on the behavior near the metal insulator transition [1,2]. In the late 1990s and early 2000s, theoretical developments allowed for calculation of transport properties of granular materials in the metallic regime; see [3] for a review. In these works a granular diagrammatic theory was used to determine the effects of weak localization [4,5], electron-electron interactions [6][7][8], and superconducting fluctuations in the presence of magnetic fields [9][10][11] on the electrical conductivity of a granular metal. This approach only considered the intergrain degrees of freedom (DOFs), and generally led to the same temperature dependences seen in homogeneous materials, albeit with a change to the relevant physical parameters, such as the effective diffusion constant.

In contrast, Lerner et. al. [12] considered superconducting fluctuations in granular metals from the perspective of the intragrain DOFs only. They predicted two crossovers in the temperature dependence of the fluctuation conductivity, Пғ f l , as one moves away from the superconducting transition temperature, T c . They assumed that, for a typical granular metal, Оҙ вүӘ О“ E T h T c , where Оҙ is the mean level spacing, О“ is the tunneling rate of electrons between grains, E T h = D 0 /a 2 is the Thouless energy of a single grain, D 0 is the intragrain diffusion coefficient, and a is the typical grain size. This assumption means that the system is in the metallic limit with the dimensionless tunneling conductance g T = О“/Оҙ вү« 1. The granular nature of the system is ensured since g g = E T h /Оҙ g T , where g g is the dimensionless conductance of an isolated grain. Lerner et. al. [12] predicted the first of these crossovers to occur as the reduced temperature, О· = (T -T c )/T c , approached О“/T c , and the second to happen at E T h /T c .

Two recent papers by Klemencic et. al. [13,14] characterised the granular transport properties in films of boron-doped nanocrystalline diamond (BNCD), where T c 4K. In [13] they measured the corrections to the electrical conductivity due to superconducting fluctuations and observed two crossovers in the behavior of the fluctuation conductivity.

As T increased, the power law behavior, Пғ f l вҲј О· Оұ , changed from Оұ = -1/2 (close-to-T c region), to Оұ = -3 (intermediate region), and back to Оұ = -1/2 (far-from-T c region). This matched Lerner et. al.'s prediction for the close-to-T c and intermediate regions, but not the far-from-T c region, where they predicted a power law with Оұ = -2.
In this paper we combine the approaches of Beloborodov et. al. [3] and Lerner et. al. [12] to consider both internal (intragrain) and external (intergrain) DOFs simultaneously.

This enables us to consistently treat the various temperature regimes: the external DOFs are needed to consider the close-to-T c to intermediate crossover; the internal DOFs are needed to consider the intermediate to far-from-T c crossover. We first tackle the problem analytically for a general granular metal, and obtain power laws for the different contributions to Пғ f l in each region. We then use the material parameters obtained by Klemencic et. al. to numerically evaluate the predictions in BNCD for comparison to experiment.

The rest of the paper is structured as follows: Section II outlines and extends the granular diagrammatic formalism to include both internal and external DOFs. The three regions of behavior in Пғ f l emerge naturally, with simple power law relations appearing deep inside each region due to the changing energy scale set by the pole of the pair propagator.

In Section III we perform the main diagrammatic calculation, which includes both internal and external DOFs. We analytically consider the limiting power law behavior of the Aslamazov-Larkin (AL), Maki-Thompson (MT), and density of states (DOS) contributions in each region, and summarise these in table I. In Section IV we discuss the different power laws produced by each contribution in each region, and their relative sizes. This allows us to make experimental predictions for superconducting fluctuations in granular metals. In Section V we compare our theory to the experimental measurements of fluctuation conductivity in BNCD by Klemencic et. al. [13]. We find that the inclusion of phase breaking is necessary to obtain semi-quantitative agreement with experiment.

div.head: II. DIAGRAMMATIC THEORY FOR GRANULAR SYSTEMS
div.p: We assume a very general form for the Hamiltonian of a granular system with superconducting correlations,
div.formula: H = i Пғ k Оҫ k c вҖ iПғk c iПғk + i Пғ k,q U i (q)c вҖ iПғk+q c iПғk + i,j Пғ k,p t kp ij c вҖ iПғk c jПғp + 1 2 i,j Пғ,Пғ вҖІ k,p V ij c вҖ iПғk c вҖ jПғ вҖІ p c jПғ вҖІ p c iПғk -О» i k,p, q c вҖ iвҶ‘k c вҖ iвҶ“q-k c iвҶ“q-p c iвҶ‘p .
div.p: The first term is the single grain free-electron Hamiltonian, where Оҫ k = k 2 /(2m)Вө is the electron energy relative to the Fermi surface; the second term describes the random scattering from impurities in a single grain; the third term accounts for tunneling between grains with the matrix element t kp ij being associated to tunneling from state p in the j th grain to state k in the i th grain. The last two terms describe electron-electron interactions. The fourth term is the Coulomb repulsion, V ij , where we neglect dependence upon intragranular momenta, q вҲј a -1 , since these are much larger than the intergranular momenta, Q вҲј L -1 , where L = N 1/3 a is the typical system length, and N is the number of grains. Finally, the fifth term is the standard s-wave BCS interaction. We may assume that the BCS interaction acts within a grain, so that our system forms a Josephson junction array for T < T c .

In this paper we focus on the corrections to the electrical conductivity due to superconducting fluctuations. Our methodology differs from the previous granular diagrammatic literature [3-7, 9-11, 15], in that we work in granular real space as opposed to lattice momentum space. Only after we have summed over the internal DOFs do we transform to the common picture of lattice momentum space. These ideas closely follow the approach of [16],
where standard rules of diagrammatics [17] are used with a few additional rules to include granularity into the problem. These new rules are as follows:
1. Each electron line receives a grain index, denoted by a Latin character (e.g. the j th grain). All internal grain labels are then summed over.

2. Each tunneling vertex, represented by a crossed circle, only allows for nearest neighbor hopping. The tunneling matrix elements are Gaussian distributed [3], analogous to the disorder present within a grain, according to
div.formula: t kp ij = 0, t kp ij t k вҖІ p вҖІ lm = пЈұ пЈҙ пЈІ пЈҙ пЈі t 2 (Оҙ im Оҙ jl + Оҙ il Оҙ jm )Оҙ k+k вҖІ =p+p вҖІ , 0, otherwise.(2)
div.p: Each pair of correlated tunneling events then carries a factor of t 2 a d , analogous to the homogeneous disorder factor (2ПҖN(0)П„ 0 ) -1 , and conserves intragranular momentum.
3. Each current vertex carries a factor of t kp ij ea/ вҲҡ N .
The averaged tunneling matrix elements can be related to О“ via Fermi's golden rule,
where N(0) is the single spin density of states per unit volume at the Fermi surface, and d is the dimensionality of the system.
The electron Green's function for granular systems maintains the same form as in the homogeneous case,
div.formula: G i (k, iОө) = 1 iОө -Оҫ k + i 2П„ sgn(Оө) .(4)
div.p: However, П„ -1 now contains scattering rates due to both impurities within a grain, П„ -1 0 , and tunneling back and forth between nearest neighbor grains,

where z is the coordination number of the lattice. This is represented by the Dyson series shown in fig. 1, where the thin solid lines denote the free electron Green's functions within a single grain, whilst thick solid lines represent electron Green's functions including impurity scattering and tunneling. The dashed lines between pairs of impurity scattering events and pairs of tunneling events denote their correlation due to averaging over the Gaussian distributions.

We now define the granular diffuson for the system, О“ ph (Q, q, iОө + iПү, iОө), via the series shown in fig. 2. Under the assumption that an electron scatters several times within a grain before tunneling, l вүӘ a (l is the elastic mean free path) -or equivalently П„ -1 0 вү« zО“ and hence П„ -1 вү« zО“ -we find the granular diffuson to have the form
div.formula: О“ ph (Q, q, iОө + iПү, iОө) = 1 2ПҖN(0)П„ 2 Оҳ(-Оө(Оө + Пү)) D 0 q 2 + |Пү| + О“О» Q .(6)
div.p: In the above, О» Q = z(1Оі Q ), where Оі Q = z -1 Оұ e iQвҖўaОұ is the structure factor, the a Оұ are the lattice vectors connecting nearest neighbor centres, and the sum is over nearest neighbors. This provides an alternative derivation of the same granular diffuson obtained by Beloborodov et. al. [3], in which the internal scattering and external tunneling events are treated on an equal footing. The details are given in the appendix.
The cooperon, О“ pp (Q, q, iОө + iПү, iОө), is also obtained in a similar manner, and has the same form as eq. 6 with an additional phase breaking rate, П„ -1 ПҶ , in the denominator
div.formula: О“ pp (Q, q, iОө + iПү, iОө) = 1 2ПҖN(0)П„ 2 Оҳ(-Оө(Оө + Пү)) D 0 q 2 + |Пү| + О“О» Q + П„ -1 ПҶ .(7)
div.p: In general we will use capital letters to denote the external momenta, Q, and lowercase letters to denote the internal momenta, q.
From the granular cooperon we now obtain the granular pair propagator,
div.formula: L(Q, q, iПү) = - 1 N(0) ln T T c,0 + ПҲ 1 2 + D 0 q 2 + |Пү| + О“О» Q + П„ -1 ПҶ 4ПҖT -ПҲ 1 2 -1 ,(8)
div.p: where ПҲ(x) is the digamma function, T c,0 is the bare transition temperature, and T c is the observed transition temperature with suppression due to phase breaking. The latter are related via
div.formula: ln T c T c,0 + ПҲ 1 2 + 1 4ПҖT c П„ ПҶ,c -ПҲ 1 2 = 0,(9)
div.p: where П„ -1 ПҶ,c is the phase breaking rate at T c . In general, following Lerner at. al. [12], we will assume that О“ вүӘ E T h T c , as well as considering the specific sub-case where E T h вүӘ T c . In the small О“ limit we may expand the digamma function to obtain,
div.formula: L(Q, q, iПү) = - 1 N(0) ln T T c + ПҲ 1 2 + D 0 q 2 + П„ -1 ПҶ 4ПҖT -ПҲ 1 2 + 1 4ПҖT c П„ ПҶ,c + ПҲ вҖІ 1 2 + D 0 q 2 + П„ -1 ПҶ 4ПҖT |Пү| + О“О» Q 4ПҖT -1 . (10
div.p: We write this in a more convenient notation as
div.formula: L(Q, q, iПү) = - 1 N(0) З«(q) + Оұ 1 (q) |Пү| + О“О» Q 4ПҖT -1 ,(11)
div.p: where
div.formula: Оұ n (q) = ПҲ (n) 1 2 + D 0 q 2 + П„ -1 ПҶ 4ПҖT ,(12a)
div.p: and
div.formula: З«(q) = ln T T c + Оұ 0 (q) -Оұ 0,c (0).(12b)
div.p: Here we have used the subscript c on Оұ 0,c (0) to denote that it is evaluated at the transition temperature. It is worth noting that З«(0) вүғ О· = (T -T c )/T c when О· вүӘ 1 and the phase breaking rate is small. This is true in all regions of behavior, except the extreme part of the far-from-T c region where T approaches 2T c .
Moving onto the sub-case, E T h вүӘ T c , we may further expand the denominator of the pair propagator to yield
As in previous literature [3], we assume the grains form a cubic lattice of side length a, so that cancel exactly in the DC conductivity limit, and so are not considered.
div.formula: О» Q = 2 d Оұ=1 [1 -cos(Q Оұ a)] .(14)
div.p: Considering small Q, we see that О“О» Q вүғ О“a 2 Q 2 , allowing us to identify D T = О“a 2 as the granular diffusion coefficient.
Finally, we note that the internal momenta are quantized according to the Neumann boundary condition corresponding to zero current normal to a grain's surface,
div.formula: n вҖў вҲҮП• = 0, (15
div.p: where П• is the electron wavefunction. We shall assume, without loss of generality, that the grains are cubic with side length a, so that the internal momenta are quantized as
div.formula: q = ПҖ a (n x , n y , n z ),(16)
div.p: where n i = 0, 1, 2, .... Clearly the above is for a three dimensional system, but the d dimensional equivalent is trivial to deduce.

Since we have quantized our internal momenta according to eq. 16, we may analyze the singular nature of the pair propagator to understand the existence of three regions of behavior as one approaches T c from above. We begin by identifying the physical energy scales in L(Q, q, iПү) as D 0 q 2 and О“О» Q , which are associated with the internal and external DOFs respectively. These appear as the dimensionless combinations D 0 q 2 /T and О“О» Q /T .
The smallest non-zero value of D 0 q 2 is E T h , so the dimensionless internal energy scale is
div.formula: E T h /T c .
div.p: Similarly, О» Q is, at most, of order unity, so О“/T c is the dimensionless external energy scale.

Looking at the most singular contributions that arise from the pair propagator, we see that when З«(0) вүғ О· вүӘ О“/T c , any non-zero q gives a significantly less singular function. However, we may consider small non-zero Q which give rise to small changes in the denominator of eq.

13, that are equally singular to the zero lattice momentum piece. We may therefore treat Q as being continuous in this region. It follows that only the external DOFs are physically relevant here, and so the system appears to be d dimensional. This is the close-to-T c region.
div.formula: Next consider О“/T c вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h /T c
div.p: , where again any non-zero q leads to less singular contributions, and hence the internal DOFs again play no role here. However, no choice of Q will generate a notable change in the propagator, so that the external DOFs are effectively unseen in this region. Therefore, neither the internal or external DOFs are physically relevant in this regime, and so the system appears to be quasi-zero dimensional. This is the intermediate region.

Finally consider E T h вүӘ О· вүӨ 1. As in the intermediate case, the external DOFs are effectively unseen. However, non-zero q can give equally singular contributions to the zero momentum term. The internal momenta can then be treated as continuous, leading to the system appearing to be d dimensional again. This is the far-from-T c region.

The above gives a concrete definition of the dimensional crossovers described in the introduction and the works of Lerner et. al. [12] and Klemencic et. al. [13]. Now that we have established the key ideas behind the granular diagrammatic method, we proceed to calculate the corrections to the electrical conductivity due to superconducting fluctuations.

div.head: III. GENERAL FLUCTUATION CORRECTIONS
div.p: By analogy to the homogeneous system, the complete set of diagrams describing the leading order fluctuation corrections to conductivity are shown in fig. 3 [18]. As we are calculating the electromagnetic response function, K ОұОІ (iв„Ұ) = в„ҰПғ ОұОІ (iв„Ұ), where в„Ұ is a bosonic Matsubara frequency, the diagrams should cancel when в„Ұ = 0. We find that this is the case working in the granular diffusive limit,
div.formula: D T Q 2 вүӘ zО“, which is equivalent to Q вүӘ a -1 . In the following we therefore replace О“О» Q with D T Q 2 .
div.p: The new features arising in the granular diagrams of fig. 3 compared to the homogeneous diagrams are the correlated fourth order tunneling events shown in figs. 3c, 3d, and 3e.

These are the leading order contributions at O(t 4 ) after averaging over the tunneling matrix elements. Other choices of event placement or correlation pairing either contribute nothing or generate higher order corrections.

We now focus on the explicit calculation of the density of states (DOS), Aslamazov-Larkin (AL), and Maki-Thompson (MT) diagrams, in that order. We assume that the system is in the granular metallic limit, Оҙ вүӘ О“ вүӘ E T h вүӘ T c , so that 1 вүӘ g T вүӘ g g . We first consider the DOS contribution in detail, constructing the electromagnetic response function in lattice real space, transforming to lattice momentum space, and treating both the internal and external DOFs simultaneously. This yields the most general form of the DOS contribution, after which we begin to consider the three limiting regimes that occur naturally in the pair
div.formula: propagator: О· вүӘ О“/T c , О“/T c вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h /T c , and E T h /T c вүӘ О· вүӘ 1.
div.p: We then summarise the expected temperature dependence of the DOS correction to the electrical conductivity deep inside each temperature region.

We then move on to the AL and MT diagrams in turn, presenting fewer mathematical details here, as the same ideas and methods are used as in the DOS calculation. In each case we first present the most general form of the response function with both sets of DOFs, before considering the different limiting behaviors. We finally summarise the temperature dependences of all corrections in each region. A complete set of our results is presented in table I at the end of this section.

div.head: A. DOS corrections
div.p: To the diagram in fig. 3a we associate the electromagnetic response function
div.formula: K ОұОұ (iв„Ұ) = - 4e 2 a 2 t 2 T 2 a 2d N i,l, m Оө,Пү k,p, q G i (k, iОө + iв„Ұ) 2 G i (q -k, iПү -iОө -iв„Ұ) Г— [G i+Оұ (p, iОө) + G i-Оұ (p, iОө)]L lm (q, iПү) Г— C il (q, iОө + iв„Ұ, iПү -iОө -iв„Ұ)C im (q, iОө + iв„Ұ, iПү -iОө -iв„Ұ) ,(17)
div.p: where Оө is a fermionic Matsubara frequency, whilst в„Ұ and Пү are bosonic Matsubara frequencies. The functions C ij (q, iОө+iПү, iОө) = 2ПҖN(0)П„ О“ pp,ij (q, iОө+iПү, iОө) are the closed cooperons at either end of the pair propagator. The labels i and i Вұ Оұ belong to the electron propagators, whilst l and m arise from the closed cooperons allowing for tunneling to a new grain, from which the pair propagator begins or ends. The associated electron lines are not seen explicitly in the diagrams, however, as their Green's functions have already been summed over in deriving the closed cooperon.
These cooperons lead to two possible sign choices for the frequencies Оө and ПүОө -в„Ұ.

We first focus on the case where Оө + в„Ұ > 0, ПүОө -в„Ұ < 0, and Оө < 0. Before obtaining the conductivity from K(iв„Ұ), we need to move to the lattice momentum picture. We start by performing the intragranular momentum sums of the electron Green's functions at the Fermi surface, leaving the sum over the small intragranular momenta, q. We then introduce the granular spatial Fourier transforms of the remaining functions via,
div.formula: L ij (q, iПү) = 1 N Q L(Q, q, iПү)e iQвҖўR ij ,(18)
div.p: where R ij = R i -R j is the relative position vector connecting the centres of grains i and j, located at R i and R j respectively. Now we perform analytic continuation of the Matsubara frequency iв„Ұ to real frequency в„Ұ + iОҙ, where Оҙ is a positive infinitesimal, to find the retarded electromagnetic response function, K R ОұОІ (в„Ұ). The conductivity tensor is then found using
div.formula: K R ОұОІ (в„Ұ) = -iв„ҰПғ ОұОІ (в„Ұ).(19)
div.p: Given that the O(в„Ұ 0 ) terms cancel, we expand K R ОұОІ (в„Ұ) to O(в„Ұ) to find the DC conductivity corrections. We further simplify the problem by only retaining the Пү = 0 piece of the Matsubara sum to neglect dynamical effects, as in the homogeneous calculation. Doing this leads to
div.formula: Пғ (1) DOS = N(0)О“a 2 e 2 4ПҖ 2 T a d N Q q ПҲ вҖІвҖІ 1 2 + D 0 q 2 + О“О» Q + П„ -1 ПҶ 4ПҖT L(Q, q, 0).(20)
div.p: Given that О“ вүӘ T c , we may neglect the О“О» Q term appearing in the digamma derivative,
div.formula: ПҲ вҖІвҖІ (x).
div.p: We may replace the Q sum by an integral, use the granular diffusive limit to replace all occurrences of О“О» Q with О“a 2 Q 2 in the integrand, and take the upper limit to be 1/a. At this point we focus on the d = 3 case in order to compare to the experimental results of [13].
We are thus left with Пғ
div.formula: DOS = О“e 2 8ПҖ 4 T a q 1 0 dQ Оұ 2 (q)Q 2 З«(q) + Оұ 1 (q)О“ 4ПҖT Q 2 . (21
div.p: We note that the alternative sign choice, Оө + в„Ұ < 0, ПүОө -в„Ұ > 0, and Оө < 0, produces an identical contribution, and so we find the correction due to the DOS to be twice that of eq. 21,
div.formula: Пғ DOS = e 2 ПҖ 3 a q Оұ 2 (q) Оұ 1 (q) 1 - 4ПҖT З«(q) О“Оұ 1 (q) arctan О“Оұ 1 (q) 4ПҖT З«(q) . (22
div.p: This is as much progress as can be made with exact analytics. We now consider the different temperature regions by taking the appropriate limits of eq. 22.

In the close-to-T c region, О· вүӘ О“/T c , only the q = 0 component gives any significant contribution. All non-zero internal momentum contributions are less singular than the zero momentum piece due to the quantized nature of q and the fact that the Thouless energy is much larger than the tunneling rate. We therefore reproduce the expected behavior analogous to homogeneous case,
div.formula: Пғ DOS = e 2 ПҖ 3 a Оұ 2 (0) Оұ 1 (0) 1 - 4ПҖT О· О“Оұ 1 (0) arctan О“Оұ 1 (0) 4ПҖT О· ,(23)
div.p: which is approximately constant for О· вүӘ О“/T c [19]. In this region the characteristic size of a fluctuating Cooper pair is much larger than the typical size of a grain. The granular system thus appears to be a homogeneous disordered medium, with the tunneling events acting as the source of disorder.

To obtain the correction in the intermediate region, О“/T c вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h /T c , we again note that only the q = 0 term gives any significant contribution, so that eq. 23 is still valid. The argument of the arctan is now small, so we may expand this to leading order to obtain
div.formula: Пғ DOS = Оұ 2 (0) 12ПҖ 4 О“ T e 2 a 1 О· .(24)
div.p: In the far-from-T c region, E T h /T c вүӘ О· вүӘ 1, we need to consider the q = 0 terms.

Since О· вү« E T h /T c , the summand will be slowly varying for small q values, and so we may approximate the sum by an integral with an appropriate upper cut-off at q = q c . A natural cut-off arises from the diffusive limit, so it appears that D 0 q 2 c = П„ -1 . However, we need to remember that the digamma derivatives decay quickly and so become small when D 0 q 2 вүҘ 4ПҖT , and so we define the cut-off via D 0 q 2 c = 4ПҖT . As the most singular behavior occurs for D 0 q 2 вүӘ 4ПҖT , we expand the digamma functions and their derivatives so that
div.formula: Оұ n (q) вүғ Оұ n (0) + D 0 q 2 4ПҖT Оұ n+1 (0),(25a)
З«(q) вүғ ln T T c + Оұ 0 (0) -Оұ 0,c (0) + Оұ 1 (0) 4ПҖT D 0 q 2 . (25b
div.p: Before performing the q integral, we are able to expand the arctan again, noting that З«(q) вү« О“/T c in this case. We therefore arrive at the integral,
div.formula: Пғ DOS = Оұ 2 (0)О“a 2 e 2 24ПҖ 6 T qc 0 dq q 2 О· + Оұ 1 (0) 4ПҖT D 0 q 2 , (26
div.p: where q = 4ПҖT /E T h , and we noted that ln(T /T c ) вүғ О· when О· вүӘ 1. This leads to the result
div.formula: Пғ DOS = Оұ 2 (0) 3Оұ 1 (0) e 2 a О“ 2 T ПҖ 9 E 3 T h 1 - О· Оұ 1 (0) arctan Оұ 1 (0) О· .(27)
div.p: This correction has the same form as the close-to-T c region, albeit with a few changes to the constants appearing alongside О·. We see that for О· вүӘ 1, the DOS contribution will again be approximately constant. This behavior can be attributed to the fluctuating Cooper pairs being much smaller than the typical grain size, and hence the system again appears homogeneous.
We have therefore shown that there are three temperature regions with different power law relations between Пғ DOS and О·,
div.formula: Пғ DOS вҲј - e 2 a пЈұ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈІ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈі const. , О· вүӘ О“ Tc О“ T О· -1 , О“ Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h Tc const. Г— О“ 2 T E 3 T h , E T h Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ 1.(28)

div.head: B. Aslamazov-Larkin corrections
div.p: The Aslamazov-Larkin diagram of fig. 3e has the electromagnetic response function,
div.formula: K ОұОІ (iв„Ұ) = - 4T a d N l,m, n,s q Пү
div.p: B Оұ,ls (q, iПү) B ОІ,nm (q, iПү)L ml (q, iПү + iв„Ұ)L sn (q, iПү) , (29
where the functions B Оұ,ls (q, iПү) represent the triangular blocks either side of the diagram.
These may be written explicitly as
div.formula: B Оұ,ls (q, iПү,iв„Ұ) = T eat 2 a d i Оө k,p G(k, iОө + iв„Ұ)G(q -k, iПү -iОө)G(q -p, iПү -iОө) Г— G(p, iОө)C li (q, iОө, iПү -Оө) C si+Оұ (q, iОө, iПү -iОө) -C si-Оұ (q, iОө, iПү -iОө) .(30)
div.p: Noting that only the pair propagator and cooperons have explicit dependence on the grain indices, we may move easily to the lattice momentum space picture, so that
div.formula: K ОұОұ (iв„Ұ) = - 4T a d N Q q Пү B Оұ (Q, q, iПү) 2 L(Q, q, iПү + iв„Ұ)L(Q, q, iПү) ,(31)
div.p: where
div.formula: B Оұ (Q, q, iПү, iв„Ұ) = 2T eat 2 a d sin(Q Оұ a) Оө k,p G(k, iОө + iв„Ұ)G(q -k, iПү -iОө) Г— G(p, iОө)G(q -p, iПү -iОө)C(Q, q, iОө, iПү -iОө) 2 .(32)
div.p: As in the homogeneous case [18,20], we may take в„Ұ = Пү = 0 inside the blocks to isolate the most singular behavior of the pair propagators. Computing the momentum and frequency sums within the granular diffusive limit yields,
div.formula: B Оұ (Q, q) = ea 2 О“N(0) 2ПҖT ПҲ вҖІ 1 2 + D 0 q 2 + П„ -1 ПҶ 4ПҖT Q Оұ .(33)
div.p: We analytically continue the Пү sum in the usual manner [18], and take the O(в„Ұ) term to arrive at,
div.formula: Пғ AL = Q q B Оұ (Q, q) 2 ПҖT Na d +вҲһ -вҲһ dz Im[L R (Q, q, z)] 2 sinh 2 z 2T ,(34)
div.p: where L R (Q, q, z) is the retarded form of the analytically continued pair propagator. By approximating sinh[z/(2T )] вүғ z/(2T ), we perform the z integration, and replacing the Q sum by an integral, we find
div.formula: Пғ AL = T e 2 6ПҖ 2 О“a q 3 Оұ 1 (q)О“ 4ПҖT З«(q) arctan Оұ 1 (q)О“ 4ПҖT З«(q) - Оұ 1 (q)О“[5Оұ 1 (q)О“ + 12ПҖT З«(q)] (Оұ 1 (q)О“ + 4ПҖT З«(q)) 2 . (35
div.p: At this point we consider the behavior of Пғ AL in the different temperature regions.
In the close-to-T c region, О· вүӘ О“/T c , we take the q = 0 term of eq. 35 to obtain
div.formula: Пғ AL = 1 8 Оұ 1 (0)T ПҖ 3 О“ e 2 a 1 О· 1/2 , (36
div.p: and hence we recover the standard d = 3 result.
In the intermediate region, О“/T c вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h /T c , we again consider just the q = 0 component, but expand in terms of Оұ 1 (0)О“/(4ПҖT О·) to obtain the leading order correction.
This results in
div.formula: Пғ AL = ПҖ 1920 О“ 2 T 2 e 2 a 1 О· 3 , (37
div.p: and we see that the AL term has the quasi-zero dimensional behavior, Пғ AL вҲј О· -3 , reproducing the result of [12]. This is due to the fluctuating Cooper pairs being of a comparable size to the typical grain.
Finally, for the far-from-T c region, E T h /T c вүӘ О· вүӘ 1, we find
div.formula: Пғ AL = 1 960 Оұ 1 (0) 3 О“ 4 ПҖ 9 T E 3 T h e 2 a 1 О· 3/2 . (38
div.p: This is to be expected given the lack of a q 2 factor in the integrand due to the current vertices.
In review, we see that the AL term also has three distinct regions, each with different dependences upon the reduced temperature,
div.formula: Пғ AL вҲј e 2 a пЈұ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈІ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈі T О“ О· -1/2 , О· вүӘ О“ Tc О“ 2 T 2 О· -3 , О“ Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h Tc О“ 4 T E 3 T h О· -3/2 , E T h Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ 1.(39)

div.head: C. Maki-Thompson corrections
div.p: Here we are interested in the diagram shown in fig. 3b. The linear response function written in lattice momentum space for this diagram is
div.formula: K ОұОұ (iв„Ұ) = 4e 2 a 2 t 2 T 2 a 3d N Q k,p, q Оө,Пү cos(Q Оұ a)G(k, iОө + iв„Ұ)G(q -k, iПү -iОө -iв„Ұ) Г— G(p, iОө)G(q -p, iПү -Оө)L(Q, q, iПү) Г— C(Q, q, iОө + iв„Ұ, iПү -iОө -iв„Ұ)C(Q, q, iОө, iПү -iОө) .
div.p: Within the granular diffusive limit cos(Q Оұ a) can be approximated by unity.

There are two types of contribution to the MT term according to the different sign choices of the Matusbara frequencies of the electron Green's functions. The three possible configurations, starting from the bottom left Green's function and moving clockwise in fig.
3b, are + + --, --++, and -+ -+. The + + -and --++ terms together produce a result, Пғ
M T , that is identical to Пғ DOS /2; the -+ -+ term produces a contribution, Пғ
div.formula: (reg2) M T
div.p: (identical to Пғ DOS /2 in the absence of phase breaking and when E T h вүӘ T c , but differs otherwise), and a more singular (anomalous) piece, Пғ
M T . Focusing on the Пү = 0 component, the second regular (non-anomalous) part can be written as,
div.formula: Пғ (reg2) M T = N(0)О“ ПҖN e 2 a 2 a d Q q Оұ 2 (q)О“О» Q L(Q, q, 0) D 0 q 2 + О“О» Q + П„ -1 ПҶ ,(41)
div.p: where we have not yet assumed anything about the size of E T h . Considering d = 3, we perform the Q integral to yield,
div.formula: Пғ (reg2) M T = S ПҶ + 1 2ПҖ 3 e 2 a q Оұ 2 (q) Оұ 1 (q) Г— 1 - Оұ 1 (q)О“П„ ПҶ 4ПҖT П„ ПҶ З«(q) -Оұ 1 (q)(1 + D 0 q 2 П„ ПҶ ) 4ПҖT З«(q) Оұ 1 (q)О“ 3/2 arctan Оұ 1 (q)О“ 4ПҖT З«(q) ,(42)
div.p: where
div.formula: S ПҶ = 1 2ПҖ 3 e 2 a q Оұ 2 (q)О“П„ ПҶ 4ПҖT П„ ПҶ З«(q) -Оұ 1 (q)(1 + D 0 q 2 П„ ПҶ ) Г— 1 + D 0 q 2 П„ ПҶ О“П„ ПҶ 3/2 arctan О“П„ ПҶ 1 + D 0 q 2 П„ ПҶ . (43
div.p: In the absence of phase breaking, S ПҶ = 0 and eq. 42 collapses to half of eq. 22 when E T h вүӘ T c , as expected. The major new feature we see in the granular Пғ
div.formula: (reg2) M T contribution is the presence of S ПҶ .
div.p: The close-to-T c behavior is given by the q = 0 piece of eq. 42. To find the intermediate behavior, we expand the arctan containing О· in eq. 42 to third order in its argument, and then take q = 0,
div.formula: Пғ (reg2) M T = 1 2ПҖ 3 Оұ 2 (0) Оұ 1 (0) e 2 a 1 + Оұ 1 (0)О“П„ ПҶ 4ПҖT П„ ПҶ О· -Оұ 1 (0) 1 3 - 4ПҖT О· Оұ 1 (0)О“ + arctan( О“П„ ПҶ ) (О“П„ ПҶ ) 3/2 . (44
div.p: Finally, the far-from-T c behavior is found by performing the q sum numerically in the case that we are unable to perform an expansion in D 0 q 2 . If, however, E T h is sufficiently small to allow this expansion, instead of taking the q = 0 piece to obtain the intermediate result, we may expand З«(q) to first order in D 0 q 2 , whilst setting q = 0 in Оұ n (q), and replace the q sum by an integral. This integral has the usual cut-off, and so yields,
div.formula: Пғ (reg2) M T = S ПҶ + 1 12ПҖ 5 Оұ 2 (0) Оұ 1 (0) 4ПҖT E T h 3/2 1 4ПҖT П„ ПҶ О· -Оұ 1 (0) Г— О“П„ ПҶ О· 1 - вҲҡ О· arctan 1 вҲҡ О· -Оұ 1 (0) ,(45)
div.p: where S ПҶ will have to be handled numerically.
It is clear that Пғ
div.formula: (reg2) M T
div.p: will only be singular in the intermediate region, similar to the DOS contribution. In the close-to-T c and far-from-T c regions, the behavior is approximately constant for small phase breaking rates. For larger phase breaking rates, the temperature dependence of П„ -1 ПҶ may become important. Moving onto the anomalous contribution, we consider only the Пү = 0 component to yield
div.formula: Пғ (an) M T = N(0)О“ ПҖN e 2 a 2 a d Q q Оұ 1 (q)L(Q, q, 0) D 0 q 2 + О“О» Q + П„ -1 ПҶ .(46)
div.p: Taking d = 3, we perform the Q integration to give
div.formula: Пғ (an) M T = 2T П„ ПҶ ПҖ 2 e 2 a q Оұ 1 (q) Оұ 1 (q)(D 0 q 2 П„ ПҶ + 1) -4ПҖT П„ ПҶ З«(q) Г— D 0 q 2 + П„ -1 ПҶ О“ arctan О“ D 0 q 2 + П„ -1 ПҶ - 4ПҖT З«(q) Оұ 1 (q)О“ arctan Оұ 1 (q)О“ 4ПҖT З«(q) .(47)
div.p: For simplicity we initially set П„ -1 ПҶ = 0, and return to the discussion of a non-zero value later. In the close-to-T c region (О· вүӘ О“/T c ), we recover the analogous d = 3 homogeneous result
div.formula: Пғ (an) M T = T 2ПҖО“ e 2 a 1 О· 1/2 . (48
div.p: TABLE I. Summary of the regional dependences of the DOS, AL, and MT corrections upon О· and the energy scales О“, E T h , and T c , when
div.formula: П„ -1 ПҶ = 0 K. Diagram О· вүӘ О“ Tc О“ Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h Tc E T h Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ 1 Пғ DOS 1 О“ T О· -1 О“ 2 T E 3 T h Пғ AL О“ T О· -1/2 О“ 2 T 2 О· -3 О“ 2 вҲҡ T E 3 T h О· -3/2 Пғ (an) M T О“ T О· -1/2 О· -1 О“ 2 T E 3 T h О· -1/2
div.p: In the intermediate region
div.formula: (О“/T c вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h /T c ) we find, Пғ(an)
div.p: which has the same power law form as the DOS term, but with a prefactor of order unity as opposed to О“/T c . Finally, in the far-from-T c region (E T h /T c вүӘ О· вүӘ 1), we obtain
div.formula: Пғ (an) M T = О“ 2 T 8ПҖE 3 T h e 2 a 1 О· 1/2 . (50
div.p: In summary, we see that the Maki-Thompson correction has the following dependence upon the reduced temperature, Пғ
div.formula: M T вҲј e 2 a пЈұ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈІ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈі T О“ О· -1/2 , О· вүӘ О“ Tc О· -1 , О“ Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h Tc О“ 2 T E 3 T h О· -1/2 , E T h Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ 1.(an)
div.p: The complete set of the DOS, AL, and MT corrections, and their temperature dependence in the three regions of behavior are summarised in table I.

Having demonstrated the existence of three distinct temperature regions in a granular system, we will now discuss the relative magnitude and temperature dependence of the various contributions, to determine which power law behaviors we expect to see experimentally.
div.formula: Пғ f l вҲј e 2 a пЈұ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈІ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈҙ пЈі T О“ 1 О· 1/2 , О· вүӘ О“ Tc 1 О· , О“ Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ E T h Tc О“ 2 T E 3 T h 1 О· 1/2 , E T h Tc вүӘ О· вүӘ 1.(52)
div.p: Including a phase breaking mechanism, П„ -1 ПҶ = 0, suppresses the anomalous MT term. If the suppression is large enough, then the anomalous MT and DOS terms may cancel almost perfectly, as they have the same intermediate behavior and are opposite in sign. In this case, the О· -3 power law of the AL term may be able to dominate in the intermediate region and therefore be observable. The exact temperature dependence of the phase breaking mechanisms present in granular systems is currently not known, and is beyond the scope of this paper. We therefore cannot rule out the possibility of a novel phase breaking mecha-FIG. 5. Experimental measurements of the fluctuation conductance from the work of Klemencic et. al. [13] with power law fittings in each region. These measurements were taken on a 329 nm thick BNCD film. The shaded areas show the approximate regions for the two crossovers.
nism that alters the temperature dependence of the anomalous MT term, such that it now produces an О· -3 power law in the intermediate region.

div.head: V. COMPARISON TO EXPERIMENT
div.p: In what follows, we make use of eqs. 22, 35, and 47 with the parameters T c , E T h , О“, a, and Оҙ being taken from the works of Klemencic et. al. [13,14], and proceed to compute these numerically to make comparison to experimental observation. We take T c = 3.8 K, E T h = 1 K (D 0 = 13.1 cm s -1 ), О“ = 2.62 Г— 10 -2 K, a = 10 -7 m, and Оҙ = 5.6 Г— 10 -3 K (this corresponds to a carrier concentration of n = 10 27 m -3 ). With these values we cannot approximate the q sum as an integral at any point, and thus the sum must be performed explicitly. Due to our assumption of the diffusive limit, D 0 q 2 вүӘ П„ -1 0 , we need only include the q = 0 term. We therefore do not include the internal DOFs in our following analysis.
In the absence of phase breaking mechanisms, we plot the theoretically expected fluctuation conductivity in fig. 4a. Comparing this to the experimental data of Klemencic et.

al. [13] in fig. 5 we see that the anomalous MT and AL contributions dominate in the close-to-T c region, producing the expected О· -1/2 behavior. In the intermediate region, the anomalous MT term overtakes the AL correction leading to an О· -1 power law. Finally, in the far-from-T c region, the anomalous MT term generates an О· -1/2 dependence, whilst dom- inating the AL contribution. Note that this far-from-T c behavior does not originate from the internal DOFs.

This prediction matches the experimental data shown in fig. 5 in the close-to-T c and farfrom-T c regions. However, the data shows a clear О· -3 power law in the intermediate region, which can be attributed to the AL term. This implies that the anomalous MT contribution must experience some form of suppression to allow for the AL power law to dominate.

Let us consider a simple mechanism of suppression in the form of a constant phase breaking rate. In real systems, П„ -1 ПҶ will also have a temperature dependence, but that is beyond the scope of this paper. Phase breaking is often neglected in 3D systems, and only used as a momentum cut-off in the 1D and 2D cases to avoid infrared divergence issues. In reality, however, phase breaking processes exist in all dimensionalities and so we should not neglect them. In fig. 6a we consider the effect of a range of constant phase breaking rates upon the total fluctuation conductivity. For large phase breaking rates, this simple mechanism suppresses the anomalous MT term sufficiently that it almost cancels the DOS correction in the close-to-T c and intermediate regions. Moving further away from the transition, the anomalous MT term begins to deviate from the DOS behavior, and starts to dominate in the far-from-T c region. This constant П„ -1 ПҶ is a simple and not entirely physical mechanism, and leads to a minimum in the fluctuation conductivity when П„ -1 ПҶ вҲј T . For comparison, let us consider a different set of physical parameters to try and shift the theoretical close-to-T c to intermediate crossover to higher values of О·. In fig. 6b we choose О“ = 0.1 K, and E T h = 3 K. Here we can see that the close-to-T c to intermediate crossover in Пғ f l occurs at a value of О· nearer to that seen in experiment. The О· -3 power law still requires a large phase breaking rate to appear, and persists over a smaller range of О·. We again observe a minimum occurring in the conductivity for large П„ -1 ПҶ . The minimum in Пғ f l that appears for large П„ -1 ПҶ is extremely small, as is the magnitude of the fluctuation data in the far-from-T c region. As a result, the shape of this data is very sensitive to the fitting applied in the high temperature region (T вү« T c ). A small change in the fitting parameters for the high temperature data can lead to a significant change in the far-from-T c fluctuation data. Therefore, obtaining the exact О· dependence of the far-from-T c region is not trivial.

In order to truly compare the theoretical and experimental results, one would need a model of the temperature dependence of П„ ПҶ , which has not been fully addressed in the literature. In particular, one would need to determine how the granularity of a material influences П„ ПҶ in the fluctuation region. We are left with two possibilities: either the anomalous MT term is suppressed such that the AL term dominates in the intermediate region, or the phase breaking rate has a novel temperature dependence that gives the anomalous MT correction an О· -3 power law. In either case, we can attribute the close-to-T c behavior to the AL and anomalous MT terms, and the far-from-T c behavior to the anomalous MT term. To fully understand the intermediate region, further study of the phase breaking rate in granular systems is required both theoretically and experimentally.

div.head: VI. CONCLUSIONS
div.p: In this paper we have demonstrated that in a metallic superconducting granular system close to and above T c , three regions of behavior exist within the fluctuation conductivity. This was shown by including both internal and external DOFs into the theoretical analysis, to reflect the character of a Cooper pair's varying size compared to the typical grain size.
This can be understood in terms of the existence of two coherence lengths in the granular system,
div.formula: Оҫ g = ПҖD 0 8T c О· , Оҫ T = ПҖD T 8T c О· ,(53)
div.p: where Оҫ g is the intragrain coherence length, and Оҫ T is the intergrain coherence length. These can be obtained from the prefactors of q 2 and Q 2 , respectively, in the pair propagator of eq. 13. The close-to-T c region occurs when Оҫ T a, the intermediate regime occurs when Оҫ T a Оҫ g , and the far-from-T c region occurs when Оҫ g a.

In order for the О· -3 power law, which is seen experimentally, to be observable, we found that the inclusion of a significant phase breaking rate was required, П„ -1 ПҶ вҲј T c -such values have been seen experimentally [21]. This is necessary to suppress the anomalous MT term, such that it cancels almost perfectly with the DOS contribution, thus allowing the AL behavior to dominate. As the phase breaking rate is increased, the observable О· -3 region becomes larger, but eventually an uncharacteristic minimum develops in the fluctuation conductivity. Looking at larger values of П„ -1 ПҶ leads to the DOS term dominating, and hence a negative Пғ f l . We note that the far-from-T c fluctuation conductivity extracted in experiment is sensitive to the high temperature fitting, and so a minimum may actually be present in the data. The assumption of a constant phase breaking rate is not realistic, and a detailed understanding of the temperature dependence of П„ -1 ПҶ may be necessary for better fitting of theory to experiment.

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. Dyson equation for the granular electron Green's function.

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. Dyson equation for the granular diffuson.

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Leading order contributions to the electrical conductivity due to superconducting fluctuations in granular systems. (a) and (c) are density of states (DOS) diagrams; (b) and (d) are Maki-Thompson (MT) diagrams; (e) is the Aslamazov-Larkin (AL) diagram. Diagrams (c) and (d)

figure.head: FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. Fluctuation conductivity contributions with no phase breaking. The black vertical lines show where the power law crossovers were seen in Klemencic et. al.'s experiment [13]. The dashed lines act as guides for power law behavior. (a) Total fluctuation conductivity; (b) DOS plus regular MT contribution; (c) AL contribution; (d) anomalous MT contribution.

figure.head: FIG. 6 .
figure.figDesc: FIG.6. Fluctuation corrections with various constant phase breaking rates, П„ -1 ПҶ . The dashed black line acts as a guide for О· -3 behavior. (a) shows the total fluctuation conductivity using parameters based upon Klemencic et. al.'s experiment[13]. (b) shows the total fluctuation conductivity using a custom set of parameters.

div.head: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
div.p: The author D. T. S. Perkins would like to thank Manjinder Kainth and Rose Davies for useful discussions and feedback regarding the contents of this paper and its presentation. D. T. S. Perkins acknowledges funding from the UK Engineering and Physical Sciences Rsearch Council (EPSRC). G. M. Klemencic wishes to acknowledge financial support by the European Research Council under the EU Consolidator Grant 'SUPERNEMS' (647471) and the UK EPSRC under the grant 'A Diamond Bridge to Phase Slip Physics' (EP/V048457/1).

org.orgName: A Diamond Bridge to Phase Slip Physics

div.head: Appendix: Derivation of the Granular Diffuson
div.p: Here we provide an alternative derivation of the granular diffuson to that of Beloborodov et. al. [3], with the inclusion of external DOFs. From the diagram in fig. 2 the first two terms form the first piece of the series, and may be written respectively as
ji = a d t 2 Оҙ ji ,
where
We first compute the diffuson self-energy
which is calculated exactly as in the homogeneous case to yield
The Dyson equation for the diffuson can then be written as, О“ ph,ji (q, iПү) = S
li + S
(2) li О ml (q, iПү)О“ ph,jm (q, iПү).

div.head: (A.5)
div.p: To solve this we move to lattice momentum space via the transform in eq. 18, to obtain
We next rewrite S
(1) ji and S
(2) ji as
which may be substituted into eq. A.6.

Finally, we assume that an electron scatters several times within a grain before tunneling to a neighboring grain, so that zО“П„ 0 вүӘ 1 and hence zО“П„ вүӘ 1. We may now expand the reciprocal of eq. A.7 to leading order in zО“П„ (Оі Q -1), and substitute the result into eq. A.6.
This yields the diffuson given in eq. 6.
[1] B. Abeles, P. Sheng, M. D. Coutts, and Y. Arie, Advances in Physics 24, 407 (1975).
[21] K. Meiners-Hagen and W. Gey, Phys. Rev. B 63, 052507 (2001).

analytic.title: Hopping conductivity in granular metals revisited

monogr.title: Methods Of Quantum Field Theory In Statistical Physics

In eq. 23, О· should really be З«(0) -the replacement of З«(0) by О· is only valid when the phase breaking rate is small. We have written О· in this equation as we are initially interested in deriving the power law behaviors in the reduced temperature, in the absence

biblStruct.note: of phase breaking. The same is true in eqs. 24, 36, 37
